comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
## Mathematical Review

# Critic Analysis — prob-01

**Problem:** *Descriptive Aspects of Structures with Countable Automorphism Groups*
(Kalociński & Slaman). Question 1.2.

**Status of attempt:** No `solution.tex` exists. No prior `docs_*.md` files are present in
the workspace (the referenced `docs_index.txt` is absent). This analysis is therefore built
from the problem statement alone.

---

## Background

We work with countably infinite first-order structures whose universe is $\N$ and whose
symbols are interpreted by elements / total functions / relations on $\N$ (a *representation*).
$\AUT(\A)$ is the automorphism group. The key definitions:

- **Computably $\AUT$-countable on a cone.** There is an oracle $C$ such that for *every*
  copy $\B \cong \A$, every $\sigma\in\AUT(\B)$ is computable from $\B\oplus C$.
- **$\Sigma^{in}_1$-definability.** $f:\A\to\A$ is $\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable from parameters
  $\bar a$ if there is a *family* of existential (computable infinitary $\Sigma_1$) formulas
  $(\phi_i)_{i\in\N}$ with $f(x)=y \iff \exists i\,\A\models\phi_i(\bar a,x,y)$. Equivalently the
  graph of $f$ is a c.e. (over a cone) union of existentially-definable sets with parameters
  $\bar a$.

Relevant cited results:

- **Theorem 2.2 (Hodges, *Model Theory* Cor. 4.2.5).** If $\A$ is countable and $\AUT(\A)$ is
  countable, then there is a *single* finite tuple $\bar a$ such that every automorphism is
  determined by its action on $\bar a$ (i.e. the pointwise stabilizer of $\bar a$ is trivial).
  This is a consequence of the topological fact that a countable closed subgroup of $S_\infty$
  has an open point-stabilizer that is trivial along some finite tuple; more precisely a
  countable Polish group with a comeager orbit / Baire-category argument forces some basic open
  set (a stabilizer of a finite tuple) to be a singleton in the group.

- **Theorem 2.3 (after Ash–Knight–Manasse–Slaman 1989).** $\A$ is computably $\AUT$-countable on
  a cone **iff** for each $\pi\in\AUT(\A)$ there is *some* finite $\bar a$ (depending on $\pi$)
  such that $\pi$ is $\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable from $\bar a$. The engine is the AKMS theorem:
  a relation $R$ is *c.e. on a cone* iff it is $\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable with finitely many
  parameters. Applying this to the graph of an automorphism converts the relativized-computation
  statement into a definability statement.

The **gap between 2.2 and 2.3** is the crux. Theorem 2.2 gives *uniform* parameters (one tuple
works for all $\pi$, and only "determines" $\pi$, not "defines" it effectively). Theorem 2.3
gives *effective* (definability) parameters but they depend on $\pi$. The Question asks whether
effectivity + uniformity can fail simultaneously, in the strong sense that **no** finite tuple
$\bar a$ can effectively define all automorphisms — and indeed for each $\bar a$ there is a
$\pi$ defeating it even when one is *allowed to add the images* $\pi(\bar a)$ to the parameters.

---

## Proof Structure

A complete solution must **construct** an explicit computably-represented structure $\A$ and
prove two clauses.

**(P1) Positive clause:** $\A$ is computably $\AUT$-countable on a cone. By Theorem 2.3 it
suffices to show: for each $\pi\in\AUT(\A)$ there is a finite tuple $\bar a$ with $\pi$
$\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable from $\bar a$. (Equivalently, build $\A$ so that any copy can compute
its automorphisms from a fixed oracle.) One must also confirm $\AUT(\A)$ is countable
(otherwise the framing collapses) — typically by exhibiting $\AUT(\A)$ concretely.

**(P2) Negative / diagonal clause:** For every finite $\bar a\subseteq\A$ there exists
$\pi\in\AUT(\A)$ such that $\pi$ is **not** $\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable from
$\bar a\cup\pi(\bar a)$. This is the hard, anti-uniformity part. It must hold for *all* finite
tuples simultaneously, so the construction must contain "enough room" / arbitrarily complex
automorphisms beyond any finite parameter horizon.

**(P3) Computable representation:** the chosen $\A$ must actually admit a computable copy
(domain $\N$, all functions/relations computable), since "computably-represented" is required.

Glue: (P1)+(P2)+(P3) together answer the Question affirmatively (such $\A$ exists).

---

## Gap Analysis

Since there is no `solution.tex`, every component below is currently an **open sub-lemma**.
These are the genuine mathematical gaps a solver must close.

**G1 — Construct the witnessing structure $\A$ (with a computable copy).**
Need an explicit $\A$ whose automorphism group is countable yet "rich enough" along an infinite
sequence of independent coordinates. A natural candidate template: a disjoint union / tree of
infinitely many finite or finitely-generated rigidifiable "gadgets" $G_0,G_1,\dots$, where each
$G_n$ contributes one independent binary (or finite) automorphism degree of freedom, but the
gadgets are *individually rigid up to a single swap* and *globally only move finitely many at a
time* in a way that keeps $\AUT(\A)$ countable. The construction must be pinned down precisely.

**G2 — Prove $\AUT(\A)$ is countable.** The framing (Theorems 2.2, 2.3) requires countable
$\AUT(\A)$. With infinitely many independent binary choices one risks $\AUT(\A)\cong
(\Z/2)^\N$, which is *uncountable*. So the construction must force automorphisms to act
nontrivially on only finitely many coordinates (e.g. $\AUT(\A)\cong\bigoplus_n \Z/2 =$ the
*direct sum*, which is countable), or otherwise restrict to a countable group. Proving exactly
which permutations preserve all relations is a real lemma.

**G3 — Prove the positive clause (P1): every $\pi$ is $\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable from finitely
many parameters.** Given G2 (each $\pi$ moves only finitely many gadgets), one needs to show that
fixing parameters witnessing the finite support of $\pi$ makes the graph of $\pi$
existentially definable (over a cone). This requires that within each gadget the swap is
existentially/computably describable once a base point is named.

**G4 — Prove the negative clause (P2): the anti-uniform diagonalization.** For an arbitrary
finite $\bar a$, exhibit $\pi$ with $\pi$ not $\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable from
$\bar a\cup\pi(\bar a)$. The subtlety: adding $\pi(\bar a)$ to the parameter set is a *gift to
the adversary*, so the gadgets must be designed so that naming $\bar a$ and $\pi(\bar a)$ still
leaves infinitely many gadgets "unaddressed", and on those unaddressed gadgets $\pi$ can encode
a non-$\Sigma^{in}_1$ (e.g. non-c.e.) amount of information. This needs (a) a coding of a
non-c.e. set into the action of $\pi$ on the gadgets outside the span of $\bar a\cup\pi(\bar a)$,
and (b) an argument that $\Sigma^{in}_1$-definability of $\pi$ from those parameters would make
that set c.e.-on-a-cone, contradicting its choice.

**G5 — Reconcile G3 and G4 (consistency of P1 with P2).** This is the conceptual heart: each
individual $\pi$ has *some* finite defining tuple (P1), yet *no* fixed tuple defines all $\pi$,
and moreover for each tuple a defeating $\pi$ exists *even after augmenting with $\pi(\bar a)$*.
The construction must make the defining tuple of $\pi$ genuinely depend on data not recoverable
from $\bar a\cup\pi(\bar a)$. One must verify there is no hidden contradiction: e.g. show that
the tuple that defines a given $\pi$ in P1 is exactly "a base point inside each moved gadget",
information *not* supplied by an arbitrary externally-chosen $\bar a$.

---

## Difficulty Assessment

- **G1/G2 (moderate–hard):** Designing relations so that $\AUT(\A)$ is the *direct sum* of the
  per-gadget groups (finite support), not the *direct product*, is delicate. A connectedness /
  "linking" relation is usually needed to force finite support, but linking can accidentally
  rigidify the whole structure (killing G4) or create unexpected automorphisms (killing G2).
  Getting countability and richness simultaneously is the standard tension in such constructions.

- **G3 (moderate):** Standard once supports are finite, but "over a cone" subtleties and the
  *family* of formulas $(\phi_i)$ must be handled carefully; one must not assume a single
  existential formula suffices.

- **G4 (hard):** This is the diagonalization against *all* finite parameter tuples with the
  $\pi(\bar a)$ handicap. The key difficulty is that the parameters $\bar a\cup\pi(\bar a)$ are
  $2|\bar a|$ points that may "spend" gadget addresses; the coding must survive this. Producing a
  non-c.e./non-$\Sigma^{in}_1$ obstruction that is robust to adding $\pi(\bar a)$ requires a
  careful priority or coding argument and is the principal research content.

- **G5 (conceptual):** Must be argued explicitly to avoid the (very plausible-looking)
  impression that P1 and P2 are contradictory. This is exactly the "gap between Thm 2.2 and
  Thm 2.3" the authors highlight; the proof must show the construction lives precisely in that
  gap.

---

## Suggested Approaches

1. **Independent-gadget direct-sum construction.** Let $\A=\bigsqcup_n G_n$ with a coordinating
   relation that forces finite support. Each $G_n$ is a small structure with $\AUT(G_n)\cong
   \Z/2$ generated by an involution $\tau_n$ whose two swapped points are *not* existentially
   distinguishable without naming an external base point. Take $\AUT(\A)=\bigoplus_n\langle
   \tau_n\rangle$. Code a target set $X\subseteq\N$ into a candidate $\pi=\prod_{n\in X}\tau_n$
   restricted to finite support along subsequences; choose $X$ (relative to the cone) to be
   non-c.e. so that $\Sigma^{in}_1$-definability fails. To beat a given $\bar a$, work on gadgets
   with index above $\max$ of all gadget-indices touched by $\bar a\cup\pi(\bar a)$.

2. **Coding via "twins" the parameters cannot resolve.** Engineer each gadget to contain a pair
   of automorphic points $\{p_n,q_n\}$ such that distinguishing them is exactly as hard as
   deciding $n\in X$; then $\pi$'s action (swap vs. fix) on the unaddressed gadgets encodes $X$,
   and $\Sigma^{in}_1$-definability of $\pi$ from $\bar a\cup\pi(\bar a)$ would give a
   c.e.-on-a-cone description of $X$, contradicting non-c.e.-ness via the AKMS direction of
   Theorem 2.3.

3. **Priority / forcing for the negative clause.** If a direct coding is fragile under the
   $\pi(\bar a)$ handicap, run a finite-injury or forcing construction building $\A$ (or building
   the bad $X$ relative to a cone) to diagonalize against the $e$-th potential family of
   existential formulas, for each finite parameter tuple enumerated.

4. **Verify the positive clause via finite support directly:** prove each $\pi$ has finite
   support, name one base point per moved gadget, and write the explicit family $(\phi_i)$ that
   defines the within-gadget swap; conclude P1 from Theorem 2.3.

---

## References

- Prior docs: **none found** (`docs_index.txt` and `docs_*.md` absent from the workspace; this
  review is from `problem.tex` only).
- W. Hodges, *Model Theory*, Cambridge, 1993 — Cor. 4.2.5 (Theorem 2.2 here).
- Ash, Knight, Manasse, Slaman, "Generic copies of countable structures", APAL 1989 — the
  c.e.-on-a-cone $\Leftrightarrow$ $\Sigma^{in}_1$-with-parameters characterization (Theorem 2.3
  here).
- Background: $S_\infty$ as a Polish group; Baire-category / topological characterization of
  countable closed subgroups; computable infinitary $\Sigma_1$ formulas; Turing cones and
  cone-uniform (Martin-style) statements.
